content:
**Leo Breiman** introduced **bagging** (bootstrap aggregating) in 1996 to stabilize unstable predictors—especially **decision trees**—by training many models on bootstrap resamples and averaging their outputs.

In 2001, Breiman published **Random Forests**, extending bagging with **random feature selection at each split**. The key insight: bagging alone leaves trees correlated when a dominant feature wins every split; forcing each split to consider only a random feature subset **decorrelates** trees so averaging cuts variance more effectively.

The method became a workhorse for **tabular ML** alongside SVMs and neural nets of the era. Open-source libraries (R **randomForest**, Python **scikit-learn** `RandomForestClassifier`/`Regressor`) standardized defaults like `max_features='sqrt'` and parallel tree fitting.

Random forests remain a strong baseline today, though **gradient boosting** libraries (XGBoost, LightGBM) often edge them on competitive benchmarks. Forests still win on simplicity, parallelism, and robust defaults—especially when tuning budget is limited.

timeline.label: Bagging (1996)
timeline.description: Breiman proposes bootstrap aggregating to reduce variance of unstable learners like deep trees.

timeline.label: Random Forests (2001)
timeline.description: Breiman adds random feature subsets at splits, defining the modern random forest algorithm.

timeline.label: Open-source adoption
timeline.description: R randomForest and scikit-learn make forests a default tabular tool with OOB scoring and parallel n_jobs.

timeline.label: GBM competition era
timeline.description: Gradient boosting libraries dominate many benchmarks; forests remain valued for speed, parallelism, and stable defaults.
